questions.question: Section 4(1) of the Criminal Justice Act 1964 defines the mens rea for murder in Irish law. Which of the following correctly states that definition?

options.A: Murder requires an intention to cause death only
options.B: Murder requires an intention to kill or to cause serious injury to any person
options.C: Murder requires foresight of a probability of death or serious injury
options.D: Murder requires subjective recklessness as to death or serious injury

questions.explanation: Section 4(1) of the Criminal Justice Act 1964 (Ireland) provides that where a person kills another unlawfully, the killing shall not be murder unless the accused intended to kill or cause serious injury. This is a statutory confirmation of the mens rea requirement for murder in Irish law, as confirmed by the Supreme Court in People (DPP) v Murray [1977]. The word 'intended' encompasses both direct intent (purpose to kill or cause serious injury) and oblique intent (virtual certainty, as confirmed in Murray). Option A is too narrow — an intent to cause serious injury (even without intent to kill) satisfies s.4(1). Option C (foresight of probability) is insufficient — probability is a lower threshold than virtual certainty for oblique intent, and in any event s.4(1) is framed in terms of intention. Option D (recklessness) may suffice for manslaughter but not murder.

questions.question: Under section 4(2) of the Criminal Justice Act 1964, where a person kills another and has the requisite intention, the fact that they did not intend to cause death to the particular victim killed does not preclude a conviction for murder. This provision codifies which common law doctrine?

options.A: The doctrine of novus actus interveniens

questions.explanation: Section 4(2) of the Criminal Justice Act 1964 provides that a person may be convicted of murder even if they did not intend to cause death or serious injury to the particular person killed, provided they had the intention to kill or cause serious injury to some person. This is a statutory codification of the transferred malice doctrine: where the accused intends to kill Person A but accidentally kills Person B instead, the malice (intention) is transferred from the intended to the actual victim. This prevents defendants from arguing that because they did not specifically target the victim who was killed, they lacked the requisite intent for murder. Options A, C, and D describe distinct doctrines: novus actus concerns causation; oblique intent concerns the nature of intention; coincidence concerns timing of actus reus and mens rea.

questions.question: Declan stabs Emer, intending to cause her serious injury, not to kill her. Emer dies from the wound. Declan claims he cannot be convicted of murder because he did not intend to kill. Is this correct?

options.A: Yes — murder in Ireland requires an intention to kill; an intention to cause serious injury is insufficient
options.B: No — s.4(1) CJA 1964 expressly provides that an intention to cause serious injury is sufficient for murder; Declan is guilty of murder
options.C: No — Declan is guilty of manslaughter because he only intended serious injury
options.D: Yes — only an intention to kill can found murder under the Irish Constitution

questions.explanation: Section 4(1) of the Criminal Justice Act 1964 is unambiguous: murder is committed where a person kills another unlawfully AND intends to kill OR to cause serious injury. The disjunctive 'or' means that an intention to cause serious injury, without any intention to kill, is sufficient for murder where death results. This is one of the key ways in which Irish murder law can catch defendants who did not intend death. Declan intended to cause serious injury (stabbing) and death resulted — the full requirements of s.4(1) are met. Option A incorrectly adds a requirement of intent to kill that is not in s.4(1). Option C wrongly concludes manslaughter — the mens rea for murder is fully satisfied. Option D introduces an incorrect constitutional requirement.

questions.question: Under the Criminal Justice Act 1964, murder carries a mandatory sentence of life imprisonment in Ireland. What is the constitutional basis for this mandatory sentence, and has it been challenged?

options.A: The mandatory life sentence is provided for in Art 40.3.2 of the Constitution and has never been challenged
options.B: The mandatory life sentence is provided for in s.2 of the Criminal Justice Act 1990 and has been challenged but upheld; the courts have confirmed that the Oireachtas has the constitutional authority to prescribe mandatory sentences, and the mandatory life sentence for murder is not disproportionate given the gravity of the offence
options.C: The mandatory life sentence violates the doctrine of proportionality and has been struck down
options.D: The mandatory life sentence applies only where the accused has a previous conviction

questions.explanation: The mandatory life sentence for murder in Ireland is provided for in s.2 of the Criminal Justice Act 1990 (replacing the pre-1990 position where the death penalty existed — abolished by the Twenty-First Amendment to the Constitution 2001, which inserted Art 15.5.2 prohibiting the death penalty). The mandatory life sentence has been challenged on grounds of proportionality and the right to have individual circumstances considered, but the Irish courts have upheld it. The Supreme Court has confirmed that the Oireachtas has the constitutional authority to prescribe mandatory sentences and that the nature of murder — the most grave crime — justifies a fixed mandatory minimum. The Irish Penal Reform Trust and various commentators have criticised the mandatory minimum, but it remains law. Options A, C and D each contain significant inaccuracies.

questions.question: What is 'constructive malice' and why was it significant in the historical development of Irish and English murder law?

options.A: Constructive malice is where malice is inferred from the use of a weapon
options.B: Constructive malice was a common law doctrine by which a person was deemed to have the mens rea for murder if they killed during the course of committing a felony, even without intending to kill or cause serious injury; it was abolished in Ireland by s.4 CJA 1964
options.C: Constructive malice is the doctrine under which transferred malice operates
options.D: Constructive malice is the modern replacement for the felony-murder rule in Irish law

questions.explanation: At common law, 'malice aforethought' for murder included 'constructive malice' — a doctrine that deemed the accused to have the requisite mental state for murder if death occurred in the course of committing a felony (the 'felony murder rule'). This meant a person could be convicted of murder even if they had no intention to kill or cause serious injury, simply because they were in the act of committing another felony when death occurred. Section 4 of the Criminal Justice Act 1964 abolished constructive malice in Ireland, replacing it with the express requirement of intention to kill or cause serious injury. In England and Wales, constructive malice was abolished by s.1 of the Homicide Act 1957. The abolition was a significant reform ensuring that murder convictions require genuine intention and not merely the fortuitous coincidence of death during a felony. Options A, C, and D mischaracterise the doctrine.

questions.question: In People (DPP) v Murray [1977] CJSC, the Supreme Court considered the mental element for murder. An accused shot and killed a Garda during an attempted armed robbery. He denied intending to kill. Walsh J and the Supreme Court addressed the mens rea requirement. What is the key principle from Murray?

options.A: Murray held that recklessness as to death is sufficient for murder in Irish law
options.B: Murray confirmed that s.4(1) CJA 1964 requires an intention to kill or cause serious injury, and that foresight of virtual certainty of death or serious injury amounts to intention within s.4(1)
options.C: Murray held that firing a gun in a confined space automatically satisfies the mens rea for murder
options.D: Murray introduced the concept of constructive intent for murder in Ireland

questions.explanation: People (DPP) v Murray [1977] is the leading Irish authority on the mens rea for murder. The Supreme Court, per Walsh J (with whom Griffin J and Henchy J agreed), confirmed: (1) Section 4(1) CJA 1964 requires proof of an intention to kill or cause serious injury; (2) Intention encompasses both direct intention (purpose) and cases where the accused knows that death or serious injury is a virtual certainty as a result of their act (oblique intent); (3) The threshold of 'virtual certainty' is significantly higher than probability or foreseeability of risk. Murray confirmed that the Irish law of murder mens rea is firmly grounded in subjective intention, not objective foreseeability or recklessness. Option A is wrong — recklessness alone does not suffice. Option C is wrong — the nature of the act does not automatically establish intent (the jury must find subjective awareness of virtual certainty). Option D is wrong — constructive intent is not a recognised Irish concept.

questions.question: Garda Wilson is killed while responding to a robbery at a post office. The accused (the robber) is convicted of murder. Section 3 of the Criminal Justice Act 1990 creates an aggravated form of murder in respect of killings of Gardaí. What additional element does s.3 require, and what is the sentence?

options.A: Section 3 requires proof that the killing was premeditated; the sentence is 40 years
options.B: Section 3 requires proof that the victim was a Garda acting in the course of their duty and that the accused knew or was reckless as to whether the victim was a Garda so acting; the sentence is a mandatory minimum 40-year period before parole eligibility (in effect, minimum 40 years to be served)
options.C: Section 3 creates an offence carrying the death penalty for killing a Garda
options.D: Section 3 requires only proof of murder of any public servant; ordinary murder sentence applies

questions.explanation: Section 3 of the Criminal Justice Act 1990 creates a specific aggravated form of murder in Irish law for the killing of: (a) a member of the Garda Síochána, (b) a prison officer, or (c) certain other protected persons, acting in the course of their duty. The prosecution must prove: (i) the ordinary requirements of murder under s.4(1) CJA 1964; (ii) that the victim was within the protected category; and (iii) that the accused knew or was reckless as to the fact that the victim was a member of the protected category acting in the course of their duty. The sentence for s.3 murder is a mandatory minimum of 40 years before any parole consideration, as opposed to the standard life sentence for ordinary murder under s.2. Option A states the threshold incorrectly — it is knowledge or recklessness, not premeditation. Option C is wrong — the death penalty is abolished. Option D misidentifies the category and the sentence.

questions.question: An accused is charged with the murder of their spouse. At trial, they claim they lost control after discovering an affair and killed in the heat of passion. Under Irish law, is provocation a complete defence to murder?

options.A: Yes — provocation is a complete defence that results in acquittal
options.B: No — provocation is not available as a defence in Irish law at all
options.C: No — in Irish law, provocation operates as a partial defence reducing murder to manslaughter, not as a complete defence to criminal liability
options.D: Yes — but only where the provocation was adultery by a spouse

questions.explanation: In Irish law, provocation (now more precisely called loss of self-control) is a partial defence to murder: it does not result in an acquittal but reduces the verdict from murder to manslaughter. The doctrine derives from People (AG) v Dwyer [1972] CJSC, where Fitzgerald J articulated the Irish common law position: where a person is so provoked by the conduct of the victim that they lose self-control, and a reasonable person in their position might similarly lose self-control, the accused should be convicted of manslaughter rather than murder. The effect of the partial defence is significant: it removes the mandatory life sentence for murder and allows the court sentencing discretion for manslaughter. In England and Wales, the Coroners and Justice Act 2009 replaced provocation with 'loss of control' as a partial defence. Options A and D overstate the defence. Option B is wrong — provocation/loss of control is available.

questions.question: What is the difference between 'causation in fact' and 'causation in law' in the context of homicide, and why does this distinction matter?

options.A: Causation in fact is determined by the jury; causation in law is determined by the judge — if the judge finds no causation in law, the jury cannot find guilt
options.B: Causation in fact (the but-for test) determines whether the accused's act was a necessary condition of the death; causation in law (operating and substantial cause; novus actus) determines whether the factual cause is legally attributed to the accused — both must be established for homicide
options.C: Causation in fact and causation in law are identical tests — both use the but-for standard
options.D: Causation in law is only relevant where there are multiple defendants each of whom partially contributed to death

questions.explanation: In homicide, causation requires two elements to be established: (1) Causation in fact — the but-for test (R v White [1910]): but for the accused's act, would the victim have died when and as they did? This establishes the minimum threshold for causation. (2) Causation in law — even where factual causation is established, additional tests determine whether that factual cause should be legally attributed to the accused: the operating and substantial cause test (R v Smith [1959]), the novus actus interveniens doctrine (Jordan, Chesire), the egg-shell skull rule (Blaue). Causation in law filters out cases where the factual cause is too remote or where an independent intervening act has broken the chain. Both must be proved beyond reasonable doubt for a homicide conviction. Option A incorrectly assigns causation to judge/jury in this binary way. Option C incorrectly equates the two. Option D incorrectly limits causation in law.

questions.question: A defendant who stabs a person in a non-vital area claims they did not expect the victim to die because the wound was 'not normally fatal.' The victim dies due to an undiagnosed haemophilia causing uncontrollable bleeding. Applying the relevant principles, is the defendant guilty of murder?

options.A: No — the defendant could not have foreseen death from a non-fatal wound, so the mens rea for murder is absent
options.B: It depends on whether the defendant intended to cause serious injury — if so, s.4(1) CJA 1964 is satisfied and the egg-shell skull rule means the defendant is responsible for the death despite the unexpected haemophilia
options.C: No — the haemophilia is a novus actus interveniens breaking the chain of causation
options.D: Yes — because any stabbing constitutes murder if death results

questions.explanation: This scenario raises two distinct issues: mens rea and causation. On mens rea: if the defendant intended to cause serious injury (which a stabbing, even in a non-vital area, may establish), s.4(1) CJA 1964 is satisfied — it is not necessary that the defendant foresaw death as the outcome. On causation: the egg-shell skull rule (R v Blaue [1975]) requires the defendant to take the victim as they find them — including pre-existing medical conditions like haemophilia. The haemophilia is a physical characteristic of the victim, not an independent intervening event — it is precisely what the egg-shell skull rule addresses. The haemophilia is not a novus actus interveniens (Option C) because it is not an independent external act; it is an inherent condition of the victim. Option A conflates foresight of death with intention to cause serious injury. Option D applies strict liability to murder, which is wrong.
